coredata.dc:title: Harnessing sulfur-doped carbon nanodots conjugated with IDO inhibitors act as a dual-mode breast cancer immunotherapy

coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent ultrasmall nanoparticles (d < 10 nm), such as carbon nanodots (CDs), are promising nanosystems for precision cancer therapy. Their optimal size allows them to diffuse within complex microenvironments, enabling drug delivery, imaging, and monitoring. Additionally, CDs can be engineered to hold inherent nanotoxicity toward cancer cells, overcoming multidrug resistance associated with conventional drugs. Nevertheless, cancer is a multifactorial disease where combinational strategies are most likely to tackle metastatic tumors and efficiently avoid recidivism. Therefore, developing multifunctional CDs that exhibit intrinsic nanotoxicity against cancer cells and drive effective antitumor immune responses is a promising approach to improving patients' response rates. Here, we developed an innovative nanosystem by conjugating N-,S-doped CDs with indoximod (IND) through a simple and cost-effective method. Our CDs-IND not only retained the advantages of bare CDs, including photoluminescence for self-tracking but also significantly controlled breast cancer progression in vivo following CDs-IND intratumoral (IT) and intravenous (IV) administration. Tumor microenvironment (TME) immune profiling revealed that CDs-IND reduced IDO expression and recruited NK, NKT, and T cells. This study underscores the potential of combining the inherent pharmacological properties of CDs with indoximod-mediated immunotherapy, offering a promising strategy for precision breast cancer treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CDs-IND synthesis. In the first step, the PEGylation of CDs is achieved by amide coupling. Subsequently, the insertion of the citraconate pendants provides the carboxyl-terminal group needed for the final reversible coupling with IND.

CDs-IND conjugates present ultrasmall size distribution and multicolor self-fluorescence. a, Atomic force microscopy (AFM) micrograph and (a’) 3D reconstruction of AFM signal. b, size distribution based on AFM heights. c, UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs (pink), CDs-PEG (green), CDs-PEG-CA (blue), and CDs-IND (orange), recorded in the 200–800 nm range. d, 3D emission spectrum of CDs-IND aqueous solution recorded in the 350–850 nm range (λEX 340–680 nm). d’, 2D emission spectra of CDs-IND in the 350–850 nm range obtained with excitation wavelengths of 370 and 450 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The CDs-IND conjugate can enter cancer cells inducing dose-dependent cytotoxic effects, which do not depend on IND pendants. a, in vitro cellular uptake of CDs-IND by E0771 breast cancer cells after 48 h of incubation, evaluated by confocal microscopy: nuclei are stained with Hoechst 33342 dye, cell membranes are stained with CellMask Orange, whereas CDs-IND are fluorescent in green. b, table reporting the half-maximal effective concentration (EC50) values calculated based on the cytotoxicity study. Cytotoxicity of indoximod (IND), CDs-PEG, and CDs-IND on E0771 (c) and NIH/3 T3 (c’), evaluated by MTT assay after 48 h of incubation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Whilst free IND does not impinge on tumor burden, CDs-IND impair in vivo tumor progression inhibiting the IDO pathway and promoting NK/NKT infiltration. Timeline (days) indicating the inoculation of E0771 cells, the treatment schedule, and the endpoint of the study (a). Mice were treated with CDs-IND (1.12 mg mL−1, 2.8 mg kg−1) or equivalent concentrations of CDs-PEG or free indoximod (IND) three days apart starting from day 7; untreated mice were used as control (N = 5–7 mice). All animals were euthanized on day 20, and tumors were harvested for further characterization. b, tracking of tumor volumes during the treatment, and (b’) average tumor volumes at the endpoint. c, quantification of CALR expression performed by spectral flow cytometry. d, TME immune profile of harvested tumors determined by spectral flow cytometry, including number of CD45+ live cells, number of T cells, natural killer (NK) cells, number of NKT cells, number of myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSC), number of tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), percentage of M1 TAMs, and percentage of M2 TAMs. e, quantification of exhaustion markers expression in the TME, including LAG-3, IDO, PD-1, and PD-L1. Data are presented as mean ± SEM, N = 5–7 mice. Statistical analysis was performed as a one-way analysis of variance followed by Tukey's test.

The administration route does not determine significant differences in pharmacological outcomes of the CDs-IND treatment. a, Timeline (days) indicating the inoculation of E0771 cells, the treatment schedule, and the endpoint of the study (a). Mice were treated by IT or IV administration of CDs-IND (1.12 mg mL−1) three days apart starting from day 7; untreated mice were used as control (N = 5–7 mice). b, trend of average tumor volume during treatment, and (c) average tumor volumes at the endpoint. d, TME immune profile of harvested tumors determined by spectral flow cytometry, including the number of myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSC), number of tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), percentage of M1 TAMs, and percentage of M2 TAMs, quantification of exhaustion markers including LAG-3 and IDO, number of NKT cells, and number of natural killer (NK) cells. Data are presented as mean ± SEM, N = 6–7 mice. Statistical analysis was performed as one-way analysis of variance followed by Tukey's test, or by Student's t-test (two-tailed) when comparing two groups.

head.ce:title: Harnessing sulfur-doped carbon nanodots conjugated with IDO inhibitors act as a dual-mode breast cancer immunotherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent ultrasmall nanoparticles (d < 10 nm), such as carbon nanodots (CDs), are promising nanosystems for precision cancer therapy. Their optimal size allows them to diffuse within complex microenvironments, enabling drug delivery, imaging, and monitoring. Additionally, CDs can be engineered to hold inherent nanotoxicity toward cancer cells, overcoming multidrug resistance associated with conventional drugs. Nevertheless, cancer is a multifactorial disease where combinational strategies are most likely to tackle metastatic tumors and efficiently avoid recidivism. Therefore, developing multifunctional CDs that exhibit intrinsic nanotoxicity against cancer cells and drive effective antitumor immune responses is a promising approach to improving patients' response rates.

Here, we developed an innovative nanosystem by conjugating N-,S-doped CDs with indoximod (IND) through a simple and cost-effective method. Our CDs-IND not only retained the advantages of bare CDs, including photoluminescence for self-tracking but also significantly controlled breast cancer progression in vivo following CDs-IND intratumoral (IT) and intravenous (IV) administration. Tumor microenvironment (TME) immune profiling revealed that CDs-IND reduced IDO expression and recruited NK, NKT, and T cells. This study underscores the potential of combining the inherent pharmacological properties of CDs with indoximod-mediated immunotherapy, offering a promising strategy for precision breast cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With 2.3 million diagnoses and approximately 670,000 deaths globally in 2022, breast cancer remains the most diagnosed malignancy and the second leading cause of death in women [1]. Despite a wide range of therapeutic options, there is an urgent need for precise and targeted strategies to improve breast cancer management and surpass standard treatment care. Cancer immunotherapy has provided several promising therapeutic approaches, beginning with the FDA approval of the monoclonal antibody trastuzumab in 1998 for targeting HER2+ tumors [2]. Over 400 clinical trials are currently evaluating the use of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) and cancer vaccines [3,4]. The most significant results have been achieved through combination therapy, which leverages the synergistic effect of immunotherapy with conventional treatments, including chemotherapy or radiation therapy [5–7]. Due to the complexity of immune suppression mechanisms in the tumor microenvironment (TME), therapies targeting specific immune-suppressive molecules, such as ICIs, are not robust enough to avoid tumor immune evasion [8,9].

Indoximod (IND), a tryptophan analog studied as a potential inhibitor of the indoleamine-2,3-dioxygenase (IDO) enzyme, plays an important role in TME immunosuppression [10,11]. Although several studies support its direct action in fighting the immunosuppressive effect of IDO [12], the ability of IND to inhibit the enzyme directly is limited [13], suggesting its action is more related to downstream pathways of IDO, involving the reactivation of mTORC1 and the modulation of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) [14,15]. However, as a monotherapy, IND has demonstrated only modest immunostimulatory activity in preclinical and clinical tests [ 16]. In contrast, treatments combining IND with other therapies, including chemotherapy [ 17–19] and immunotherapy [ 14,20], have achieved encouraging results, leading to advanced stages of clinical trials [ 21,22] and validating its potential as an immune adjuvant. The combination of IND with chemotherapeutics inducing immunogenic cell death (ICD), such as doxorubicin, resulted in remarkably favorable, especially if a nanoparticle was used to co-deliver the two agents to the target tissue [23,24]. By promoting the release of damage-associated-molecular patterns (DAMPs) at the tumor site [25], ICD promotes a local inflammatory state that stimulates innate and adaptative immune responses [26], thereby synergizing with immunotherapy [27].

In addition to serving as delivery systems, nanosystems can be engineered to exhibit intrinsic antitumor properties [28], offering a safer alternative to classic chemotherapeutics and avoiding their potential off-target toxicity [29]. Among engineered nanosystems, carbon nanodots (CDs), an emerging class of zero-dimensional (d < 10 nm) biocompatible and emissive carbon allotropes, hold several advantages including low-cost controlled synthesis, high water dispersibility, surface chemistry suitable for functionalization, and interesting optical properties, such as tunable multicolor fluorescence emission that confers self-tracking ability by bioimaging [30,31]. Moreover, their small size allows them to spread throughout the body, including within the complex and dense TME, which often acts as a barrier to the penetration of nanomedicines. This characteristic facilitates direct action on the tumor parenchyma and may help bypass multidrug resistance, which commonly occurs when larger nanosystems (greater than 20 nm) struggle to diffuse effectively and fail to accumulate at the desired site. Despite the growing interest in CDs due to their particular characteristics, limited research has been reported to date on the use of these nanostructures to elicit innate and adaptive antitumor immune responses [32,33], leaving this strategy still underexplored. Our N-,S-doped CDs have shown selective anticancer activity toward breast cancer cells, through a mechanism involving increased expression of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in tumors compared with healthy cells [34,35]. The overproduction of ROS leading to cell death has been associated with enhanced antitumor immune response in literature, suggesting their central role in ICD mediated by various agents [25,36–38].

Based on these insights and the tunability of CDs, we developed an innovative nanosystem consisting of CDs-indoximod conjugates (CDs-IND), designed to leverage the inherent tumor-targeted toxicity of CDs combined with immune stimulation, using IND as an immune adjuvant. The anticancer properties of CDs-IND were investigated using both in vitro and in vivo breast cancer models followed by extensive TME characterization. CDs-IND effectively enhanced the immune response against breast cancer in vivo. Finally, we demonstrated the similar effectiveness of CDs-IND administrated locally and systemically to ensure the optimal delivery and efficacy of the developed nanotechnology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of homogeneous CDs-IND conjugates with combined pharmacological properties
ce:section.ce:para: CDs (5.3 ± 0.4 nm in diameter, Fig. S1) were synthesized following our methodology [34]and subsequently coupled with IND through a biocompatible linker consisting of a poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) chain and a citraconate pendant. In the first synthetic step, the presence of several carboxylic groups on the surface of CDs allowed for easy functionalization with bis-amine PEG (Mw 2 kDa) through amide coupling, using EDC and NHS as activating agents (Fig. 1 ). In a separate experiment, we previously verified the complete permeation of free PEG-2000 across the dialysis membrane under identical conditions, ensuring the effective purification of the CDs-PEG conjugates while optimizing reaction yield. Indeed, using a membrane with an MWCO of 2 kDa, some ultrasmall CDs can pass through the membrane, reducing the overall yield of the reaction. The potential crosslinking resulting from the reaction between the two amine end-chains of PEG and two CDs was avoided by using a large excess of PEG. This excess prevented undesired network formation, which could alter the physical properties of the nanosystems. Engineering the CDs surface with discrete PEG2000 chains (Flory's radius ∼ 1.2 nm) is expected to improve the bioavailability of the nanosystem after intravenous administration by protecting the nanoparticles from opsonization and protein corona formation, without significantly altering the 0D nature of the nanosystem [39]. Indeed, this strategy increases the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs by only a few nanometers, preventing rapid excretion due to their size, which is close to the renal cutoff of 5.5 nm. After degradation, free CDs are expected to be successfully excreted through renal clearance and hepatic metabolism.

Next, the primary amine end-chain of PEGylated CDs (CDs-PEG) was functionalized with citraconic anhydride, providing a free carboxylic terminal group suitable for reacting with other amine functions [40]. The secondary amine of IND was then exploited for amide coupling with these carboxylic terminal groups, again in the presence of EDC and NHS. At each synthetic step, the product was isolated, purified by dialysis (MWCO 1 kDa) against water, and freeze-dried to obtain a dry powder.

The feasibility of the surface functionalization of CDs was established by comparing the FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra of CDs-IND and its precursors, confirming that the conjugate had been properly formed (Supplementary Information, sections 2–3, Fig. S2-S3).

The amount of PEG chains in the CDs-PEG conjugate was calculated both by 1H NMR, using acetone as an internal standard (Fig. S3), and by quantifying the amount of primary amine functions using the ninhydrin assay. The latter value directly correlates to the amine end chains of PEG pendants, as the presence of free NH2 groups on bare CDs was negligible (FigS1b’). Combining both methods, the amount of PEG chains in the CDs-PEG conjugate was determined to be 0.3 μmol mg−1 CDs-PEG. Considering the ratio between the diagnostic peaks of citraconate and PEG in the 1H NMR spectrum of the CDs-PEG-CA conjugate (Fig. S3), we found that 45% of PEG chains were functionalized with the citraconate pendant. Similarly, by analyzing the ratio between the aromatic hydrogens of IND and PEG (Fig. S3), we determined that 13% of the side chains presented IND as a terminal moiety, corresponding to 7.7 μg of IND per mg of nanosystem.

We obtained homogeneous and stable CDs-IND conjugates, with an average diameter of 6.6 ± 1.7 nm and a narrow size distribution (Fig. 2a -a’, 2b). The conjugate slowly released the drug payload over time, without exhibiting a burst effect (∼8% within 8 h), reaching a maximum release of 18% after 10 days under acidic conditions (Fig. S4b). This drug release corroborated FTIR and 1H NMR data which suggest the formation of citraconic amide bonds between IND and CDs.

The surface functionalization of CDs did not impair their optical properties, including a broad absorption spectrum extending across the UV–visible region, which was also displayed by all the derivatives. The absorption spectra of CDs-PEG, CDs-PEG-CA, and CDs-IND maintained the same profile of plain CDs, with only a slight red-shift of the main peaks at 340 nm and 550 nm, and a loss in the definition of the 460 nm peak (Fig. 2c). The optical absorption of CDs-IND resulted in multicolor fluorescence emission, with main emission peaks in the blue-green region, specifically at 445 nm and 540 nm (λex 370 nm and 450 nm respectively), and a tail extending into the red region (Fig. 2d-d’). The fluorescence quantum yield of these CDs varies from 6% in the blue/green to approximately 1% in the NIR region [34]. Thus, the inherent photoluminescence of CDs-IND confers self-tracking ability to the nanosystem, which is useful for performing in vitro evaluations via bioimaging.

The optical properties of CDs-IND offer valuable insights into their colloidal stability and interactions in biological media, such as fetal bovine serum (FBS)-supplemented DMEM (Fig. S4 c-d”). Fluorescence and absorbance measurements serve as indirect yet reliable indicators of stability, as aggregation and sedimentation typically cause a measurable decrease in signal intensity [41]. Additionally, CDs are highly sensitive to surface interactions, and their fluorescence emission can shift upon interacting with biomolecules like proteins in FBS [42,43]. This optical response is widely recognized as an effective probe for monitoring surface dynamics and nanoparticle interactions. Given the limitations of dynamic light scattering (DLS) in strongly fluorescent ultrasmall nanoparticles, optical measurements complement traditional size characterization methods by providing evidence of colloidal behavior and stability under varying conditions. As shown in Fig. S4 c-d”, the absorption and emission spectra of CDs-IND in FBS-enriched DMEM remained unchanged over 24 h of incubation, suggesting minimal surface adsorption and aggregation possibly due to PEGylation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Indoximod does not affect the in vitro cytotoxicity profile of the CDs-IND conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The inherent green photoluminescence of CDs-IND was exploited to evaluate their cellular internalization in a murine mammary cancer cell line (E0771). Micrographs showed a strong self-fluorescence signal from CDs-IND within cells after 24 h of incubation, with peak uptake observed at 48 h. This fluorescence did not co-localize with DAPI blue fluorescence and CellMask Orange membrane stain (Fig. 3a), suggesting that CDs-IND preferentially localizes within the cytosol rather than nuclei. This preference is likely due to size constraints and surface properties that favor cytoplasmic retention. The internalization slightly decreased after 72 h (Fig. S5), indicating that CDs-IND can enter and be retained in cancer cells for up to 72 h. According to the literature, the cellular entry mechanism for these nanostructures is predominantly passive [44]. Notably, the self-tracking ability of the nanosystem allowed for cellular uptake studies without requiring the addition of external fluorescent dyes, highlighting the potential of CDs-IND for bioimaging in vitro, using confocal microscopy. Upon internalization, the CDs-PEG conjugate exhibited a dose-dependent cytotoxic effect in both the cancerous E0771 cell line and the healthy NIH/3 T3 cell line, with half-maximal effective concentrations (EC50) of 660 μg mL-1 and 1138 μg mL-1, respectively (Fig. 3b). Interestingly, functionalization of CDs significantly improved their cytocompatibility with both tested cell lines compared to unmodified CDs-PEG (Fig. 3c-c’).

At concentrations up to 0.5 mg mL-1, CDs-PEG and CDs-IND followed similar cell viability trends. However, at higher concentrations, CDs-PEG exhibited markedly greater cytotoxicity, especially against the E0771 cell line. This behavior supports previous findings regarding the intrinsic anticancer properties of CDs, which are attributed to their ability to disrupt ROS signaling pathways in cancerous and pre-cancerous cells [34,35]. In contrast, free IND showed total cytocompatibility at all tested concentrations, consistent with existing literature [ 45]. These results highlight the dual functionality of CDs-IND: not only do they serve as drug delivery systems, but they also demonstrate intrinsic anticancer activity, reducing reliance on traditional cytotoxic chemotherapeutics and harmful radiation therapy [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo synergistic reduction of tumor burden by CDs-IND conjugates through NK/NKT activation and IDO pathway regulation combined with CDs intrinsic anticancer properties
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-IND conjugate was designed to control breast cancer progression by integrating the inherent anticancer properties of CDs with the stimulation of the immune system via the IDO pathway. This expected synergic anti-tumor effect was evaluated in vivo in mice bearing the E0771 syngeneic mammary adenocarcinoma mouse model. E0771-bearing C57BL/6j mice were treated with intratumoral injections (IT) of either free IND, CDs-PEG, or CDs-IND according to the schedule shown in Fig. 4a. In addition, the changes in body weight were monitored to assess treatment tolerability and safety (Fig. S6). On day 20 after tumor inoculation, the average tumor volume of mice treated with CDs-IND was significantly lower compared to all other treatment groups, resulting in a 3.5-fold decrease in average tumor volume compared to the untreated control. No significant differences were observed among the IND, CDs-PEG, and untreated groups (Fig. 4b’ – Fig. S7). The expression of calreticulin (CALR) on dying cells was quantified to assess whether the observed control of tumor growth could be attributed to ICD induced by CDs. Among ICD-relevant DAMPs, CALR is widely recognized as a key prognostic biomarker, reflecting the activation of a clinically relevant anticancer immune response across various malignancies [47]. As shown in Fig. 4c, a slight increase in CALR expression was observed across all treatment groups; however, this increase was not statistically significant compared to the control. These findings suggest that the antitumoral effect demonstrated by CDs-IND is not only solely attributable to the inherent cytotoxicity of CDs toward cancer cells. Instead, this cytotoxicity may contribute to amplifying the synergistic effect of the combination with IND by promoting immune activation. To determine if the enhanced anticancer effect of CDs-IND was related to a boosted antitumoral immune response, the TME immune profile of harvested tumors was characterized using spectral flow cytometry (Fig. S8). Fig. 4d shows that conjugation of IND to CDs resulted in a strong immune response, with CDs-IND-treated tumors exhibiting elevated levels of various immune cell types, including T-cells, natural killer (NK) cells, natural killer T cells (NKT) cells, and tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), which were mostly polarized to a pro-inflammatory (M1) phenotype, compared to controls. However, myeloid-derived suppressor cell (MDSC) levels were also significantly different from those in non-treated tumors, potentially hindering complete tumor remission following CDs-IND administration. Nevertheless, exhaustion markers such as LAG-3, IDO, PD-1, and PD-L1 were remarkably lower in CDs-IND-treated tumors, indicating reduced immune suppression in the TME after treatment (Fig. 4e).

Overall, the immune profile highlights the ability of CDs-IND treatment to arm a robust immune response against the tumor. The indiscriminate increase of immune mediators in the TME precludes discerning between pathways directly stimulated by treatment and those ascribable to secondary feedback responses. However, it is noteworthy that significant differences between non-conjugated CDs-PEG and indoximod-conjugated CDs-IND were observed in terms of levels of NK and NKT cell infiltration and IDO expression levels. Since the immune response induced by unconjugated CDs-PEG and their intrinsic antitumoral properties were insufficient to limit tumor growth, our data suggest that the divergent immune pathways of the two treatments are responsible for the observed difference in anticancer effects. NK and NKT cells are critical components of innate immunity and are gaining attention in cancer immunotherapy due to their role in controlling tumor growth [48,49]. Their infiltration into tumor tissue correlates with better disease prognosis [50]. In the TME, NK cell function can be impaired by immunosuppressive pathways [51], including IDO [52,53], which inhibits the NK cell cytotoxicity and accumulation in the tumor stroma [54,55]. IND treatment, which reactivates mTORC1 can restore NK cell antitumoral function by alleviating metabolic impairment [56]. Moreover, IDO downregulation has been shown to enhance cancer cell sensitivity to NK in vitro and promote NK cell accumulation in the tumor stroma in vivo, in a breast adenocarcinoma model [57]. These findings align with our results that indicate that the increase in NK and NKT cell infiltration and reduced IDO expression impact the outcome of CDs-IND treatment. The downregulation of IDO in CDs-IND-treated mice can be attributed to the presence of IND in the nanosystem structure, which inhibits the IDO downstream pathway [14]. Nevertheless, the TME immune profile of IND-treated tumors was surprisingly similar to untreated controls, suggesting that the tested concentration (40 μM) of IND as a single agent is ineffective in eliciting an antitumoral immune response or relieving TME immunosuppression. These findings, which may seem inconsistent with in vitro evaluations of IND activity on T cells [12], are coherent with the modest immunostimulatory effects observed in clinical trials when used as monotherapy [16]. Despite varying reports on the effect of IND used as a single agent on IDO expression levels [58], clinical trials combining IND with other therapies have shown clear IDO down-regulation within TME [21], supporting our findings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vivo immune effects are administration-route-independent
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the encouraging results obtained from the IT administration of CDs-IND, a deeper investigation was necessary to establish the reproducibility of the antitumoral effect of CDs-IND treatment following systemic administration. The use of the IT administration route has been questionable due to its limited application. Therefore, we evaluated the impact of intravenous (IV) administration of CDs-IND compared to IT-treated and untreated groups in E0771-bearing mice. The treatment followed the same schedule as previously described (Fig. 5a), with IV administration performed by injection into the tail vein of the mice. As observed for the IT administration route, the antitumor effects in terms of tumor burden reduction due to the CDs-IND conjugate are much more remarked if compared with the simple treatments with the base CDs and free IND (Fig. S9). As reported in Fig. 5b, IV treatment with CDs-IND produced results very similar to IT treatment in terms of tumor growth control, leading to a 4-fold decrease in average tumor volume compared to the untreated control at day 20 (Fig. 5b-c). The comparable outcomes of the two therapeutic strategies reinforce the effectiveness of CDs-IND in mitigating tumor growth, suggesting that the synergic combination of CDs' intrinsic anticancer activity and IND-mediated immune stimulation produced the same effect after both IT and IV administration. The IV-treated mice presented differences in TAMs and T cell activation, primarily presenting an anti-inflammatory (M2) phenotype and a significantly higher percentage of LAG-3 and PD-1 exhaustion markers on T-helper lymphocytes (Fig. 5d) compared to the TME of the IT-treated group. Nevertheless, these immune cell types do not seem to play a major role in the mechanism through which CDs-IND affect tumor progression, as their presence did not affect the overall antitumor effect. Both groups exhibited the same low percentage of IDO expression and similar amounts of NK and NKT infiltrating the TME. Thus, the central role of these two pathways in the overall antitumor effect is corroborated by this experimental set, reinforcing our previous interpretation.

The effectiveness of CDs-IND treatment after IV administration proves that the antitumoral effect of the nanosystem is not limited to its administration at the site of action. This finding validates the potential use of this nanotherapeutic tool for more accessible systemic routes of administration.

This study has demonstrated the successful synthesis and application of N-,S-doped carbon nanodots conjugated with IND, forming a novel and effective nanosystem (CDs-IND) for breast cancer therapy. Carbon nanodots were conjugated with IND through a simple and cost-effective synthetic protocol, resulting in a smart nanodevice that retains the advantageous chemical, physical, and optical properties of the bare CDs. The unique properties of the CDs-IND conjugate, including their bright photoluminescence, allow for self-tracking and potentially significant applications in liquid biopsy and image-guided laparoscopic surgery, enhancing precision in cancer treatment.

CDs-IND showed a dose-dependent in vitro antitumoral effect, primarily attributable to the established antitumoral properties of the CDs component, although these properties are partially mitigated by surface functionalization. We demonstrated for the first time that combining the inherent pharmacological properties of N-,S-doped CDs with IND-mediated immunotherapy significantly controlled breast cancer progression in vivo. Both IT and IV administration of the CDs-IND conjugate resulted in a significant reduction of tumor burden in mice, whereas neither CDs-PEG nor free IND managed to slow the exponential growth of tumor cells, even when locally administrated at the tumor site.

The extensive analysis of the TME immune profile, conducted to establish a relationship between the nanosystem-mediated antitumoral activity and enhanced immune response, highlighted the involvement of NK and NKT cells, attributable to the reduced expression of IDO, in the antitumor effect mediated by CDs-IND. Indeed, systemic administration of the nanosystem controlled tumor progression similarly to intratumoral administration, despite a more immunosuppressive microenvironment in which only NK, NKT, and IDO pathways were comparable with those detected in IT-treated mice. These findings indicate that the CDs-IND conjugate not only exerts direct antitumoral effects but also stimulates an immune response, validating the concept of combining nanomedicine with immunotherapy for breast cancer treatment.

The systemic efficacy observed suggests that this approach could offer a versatile and less invasive alternative to surgical or localized treatments of metastatic breast cancers. Future research should focus on exploring the application of this nanosystem in other types of cancer to further establish its broad-spectrum efficacy. Investigations into the long-term stability and biocompatibility of CDs-IND will be crucial for advancing this technology toward clinical application.

In conclusion, the promising results of this study pave the way for further development and clinical translation of combined nanomedicine and immunotherapeutic strategies, potentially revolutionizing the treatment landscape for breast cancer and beyond.

Carbon nanodots (CDs, 50 mg, 1.994 ×
10 17
particles), prepared following the protocol described by Mauro et al., 2022 [34], and an excess of poly(ethylene glycol) bis(amine) (Mw 2 kDa, 200 mg, 6.022 ×
10 19
chains) were solubilized in 5 mL of ultrapure water. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.8 and EDC (19.2 mg, 0.1 mmol) and NHS (11.5 mg, 0.1 mmol) were added under stirring. The mixture was kept reacting overnight at pH 6.8. The product (CDs-PEG) was purified by dialysis against ultrapure water, using a SpectraPor® Pre-wetted RC Tubing (MWCO 1 kDa), and freeze-dried. Subsequently, 178 mg of CDs-PEG were solubilized in 8 mL of ultrapure water and the pH was adjusted to 9. In parallel, 357 μL of citraconic anhydride (3.97 mmol) were diluted to 2 mL with tetrahydrofuran under an inert atmosphere and added dropwise to the CDs-PEG solution under stirring, while maintaining the pH in the 8.5–10 range with NaOH 1 M. The reaction was kept under stirring at pH 9 for 3 h. The product was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 72 h, using a SpectraPor® Pre-wetted RC Tubing (MWCO 1 kDa), and freeze-dried. The product (CDs-PEG-CA) was obtained with a 77.8% yield. Finally, IND (12 mg, 55 μmol) was solubilized in 10 mL of ultrapure water by sonication and slight heating, and CDs-PEG-CA (88 mg), previously solubilized in 2 mL of ultrapure water, were added. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.8, and EDC (8.5 mg, 55 μmol) and NHS (6.3 mg, 55 μmol) were added under magnetic stirring. The mixture was kept reacting overnight, then the product was purified by dialysis against ultrapure water, using a SpectraPor® Pre-wetted RC Tubing (MWCO 1 kDa). The freeze-drying of the pure product, CDs-IND, resulted in a dark powder with a 70% yield.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed in the 4000–400 cm−1 range, using a PerkinElmer Spectrum Two IR spectrometer (Waltham, MA). Samples were prepared as KBr pellets and dried under vacuum before the analysis.
1H NMR spectra were recorded with an Advance II spectrometer (Bruker Biospin) operating at 400.15 MHz.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) micrographs were obtained using a Bruker FAST-SCAN microscope equipped with a closed-loop scanner (X, Y, Z maximum scan region: 35 μm, 35 μm, 3 μm, respectively). The measurements were performed in soft-tapping mode using a FAST-SCAN-A probe (apical radius 5 nm).

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded with a double-beam spectrophotometer Shimadzu UV-2401PC, operating in the 200–800 nm range (1 nm bandwidth). Samples of CDs, CDs-PEG, CDs-PEG-CA, and CDs-IND were prepared as aqueous solutions and sonicated before the analysis.

The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs-IND was recorded using a JASCO FP-85 spectrofluorometer operating in 3-D acquisition modality in the range 350–850 nm (λEX 340–670 nm). The analysis was repeated in 2-D acquisition modality at 350–850 nm and at excitation wavelengths of 370 and 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of PEG chains in CDs-PEG
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantity of NH2-terminal PEG chains was determined spectroscopically, using ninhydrin as an indicator. Briefly, 1 mL of 1 % (w v −
1 ) ninhydrin solution (2.5 % EtOH) was added to 1 mL of a 0.2 mg mL−1 solution of CDs-PEG. The 570 nm absorbance of the mixture was compared to those of glycine standard solutions (0–10 mM) treated similarly.
The amount of PEG in CDs-PEG was also confirmed by 1H NMR using acetone (2.5 μL) as an internal standard (i.s.).

The murine mammary cancer cell line E0771 was cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10% v v−1) and penicillin/streptomycin (100 unit mL−1).

The same cell line was used to establish a syngeneic mammary adenocarcinoma model, by orthotopic subcutaneous injection in C57BL/6j mice. Female C57BL/6j mice (10–12 weeks old) were purchased from Instituto Gulbenkian Ciência (Portugal) and housed animal facility of the Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Lisbon. The animal procedures were performed in compliance with the Portuguese competent authority for animal protection, Direcção Geral de Alimentação e Veterinária, guidelines (Reference 0421/000/000/2021). Animals were housed under a 12-h light, 12-h dark cycle, with food and water available ad libitum and handled in compliance with the National Institutes of Health (NIH) guidelines and the European Union (EU) rules for the care and handling of laboratory animals (Directive 2010∖63∖EU). The inoculation was performed by injecting 100 μL of a suspension of E0771 cells (1.0 × 106 cells) in a 1:1 mixture of PBS/Matrigel® at the animal's fourth inguinal mammary fat pad. Mice weight and tumor size were monitored three times a week for the entire study duration. Tumors were measured using a digital caliper, and their volume was calculated by applying the ellipsoid volume formula.

The cytotoxicity of CDs-IND was assessed in vitro on a breast cancer cell line (E0771) and on a healthy cell line (NIH/3 T3) using the MTT assay. In detail, cells were seeded on a 96 well-plate at 3.5 × 103 cells per well density and cultured in complete DMEM at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After 24 h the cell culture medium was replaced with 150 μL of either CDs-IND solutions in DMEM (0.1–2 mg mL−1), or CDs-PEG or free IND at an equivalent concentration. Cells were incubated for 48 h, then the cell culture medium was retrieved, and the wells were washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4). Cells were incubated with a thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide solution in DMEM (50 μL, 0.5 mg mL−1) for 2 h, and then 100 μL of DMSO were added to resuspend the formed crystals. The absorbance of the samples at 570 nm was measured using a Varioskan Lux microplate reader (Thermofisher Scientific). Untreated cells were used as a negative control, and cells treated with tween 20 were used as a positive control. Data were reported in terms of percentage cell viability compared to the control (viability 100%). The experiment was performed twice in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake of CDs-IND
ce:section.ce:para: E0771 cells were seeded in an ibidi® μSlide-8well (9.0 × 103–3.0 × 103 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h, then the culture medium was replaced with 150 μL of a 1.5 mg mL−1 solution of CDs-IND, or with fresh medium for controls. After 24, 48, and 72 h of incubation, the culture medium was removed, cells were washed with PBS, and fixed with 4 % buffered formaldehyde. Nuclei were stained using the Hoechst 33342 dye (150 μL, 5 μg mL−1). Cell membranes were stained using the CellMask Orange Membrane Stain (150 μL, 1:1000). Confocal fluorescence microscopy was performed using a Leica TCS SP8 (Leica Microsystems CMS GmbH, Mannheim, Germany) inverted microscope (DMi8) with a 63× oil (1.4 numerical aperture) HC PL APO objective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo evaluation of the anticancer activity of CDs-IND
ce:section.ce:para: Seven days after the tumor inoculation, tumor-bearing mice presenting palpable masses of ∼67 mm3 average volume were randomized into four treatment groups (N = 5–7 per group) and treated by intratumoral (IT) administration of CDs-IND 1.12 mg mL−1 (50 μL, 2.8 mg kg−1) or equivalent amounts of CDs-PEG or free IND (8.6 μg mL−1, 40 μM). The treatment was repeated 5 times, three days apart. Untreated mice were used as negative controls. Tumor size and mice weight were monitored three times a week. All mice were euthanized the day after the last treatment (day 20), and spleens and tumors were harvested to extract single-cell suspensions for subsequent experiments. The experiment was repeated twice.

In a parallel experimental set, mice were treated by intravenous (IV) administration of CDs-IND (1.12 mg mL−1, 100 μL, 5.6 mg kg−1), following the same treatment schedule (N = 5). At day 20, mice were euthanized, and the spleen and the tumor were harvested to extract single-cell suspensions for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immune profile characterization through flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: Tumors and spleens were isolated, directly after euthanasia, and placed in cold sterile PBS 1× (pH 7.4). Tumor single-cell suspensions were obtained by mechanical disruption of the tissues and enzymatic digestion for 20 min at 37 °C. Enzymatic digestion solution was prepared in RPMI medium with 0.5% (w v−1) BSA, 0.1% (w v−1) collagenase type II, 0.1% (w v−1) dispase, and DNAse. After digestion, the cell suspension was filtered through a 70 μm filter (BD Biosciences) with cold PBS 1× (pH 7.4) to remove the debris. The cell suspension was centrifuged at 300 g for 5 min at 4 °C and washed with cold PBS 1× (pH 7.4). The cell pellet was resuspended in cold PBS 1× (pH 7.4), and cells were seeded in U-bottom 96 well plates (3.0 × 106 cells per well) and stained with Ghost Dye™ Red 780 for cell viability. Cells were then washed with FACS buffer (0.5% (w v−1) BSA, 2 mM EDTA in PBS) and stained for surface markers: CD45 (AF532, clone 30-F11), CD3 (FITC, clone 17A2), CD4 (perCP-Cy5.5, clone GK1.5), CD8 (AF700, clone 53–6.7), IDO (PE, clone Mido-48), CD279 (SB600, clone J43), CD107b (AF647, M3/84), CD25 (PECy5, clone PC61), NK1.1 (BV570, clone PK136), CD274 (BV711, clone 10F.9G2), CD223 (PE-Cy7, clone eBioC9B7W), CD11b (BV785, clone M1/70), CD11c (PECy5, clone N418), F4–80 (BV711, clone BM8), MHCII (AF700, clone M5/114.15.2), CD206 (PECy7, clone C086C2). All staining was performed in the FACS buffer. To analyze FOXP3 expression, cells were further fixed and permeabilized using the eBioscience™ FOXP3/Transcription factor staining buffer set, according to the manufacturer's protocol, and stained with FOXP3 (eFluor450, clone FJK-16 s). Cells were analyzed using a Cytek Aurora (Cytek). Data analysis was performed using FCS Express (De Novo Software).

Statistical analysis was performed using Student's t-test (two-tailed) for comparing two groups, and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey's post-hoc analysis for comparing sets of data composed by multiple groups. Statistical calculations were performed using the Microsoft®-Excel data analysis tool and the software package GraphPad Prism (V.7.03). Comparisons were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05 (∗), p < 0.01 (∗∗), p < 0.001 (∗∗∗), and p < 0.0001 (∗∗∗∗).

Animals were housed under a 12-h light, 12-h dark cycle, with food and water available ad libitum and handled in compliance with the National Institutes of Health (NIH) guidelines and the European Union (EU) rules for the care and handling of laboratory animals (Directive 2010\63\EU).
